key_insight: For lung CT phenotyping, the best-performing pipeline depends more on the task than on a single universally superior model: segmentation matters most for tumor volume and stage, while the classifier head matters most for survival, histology, and age prediction.
clinical_relevance: A two-stage, non-end-to-end workflow can be a practical default for small cohorts, with Curia plus tumor segmentation and CatBoost giving the strongest overall balance across the main clinical tasks.
method: Benchmarked 5 feature extractors, 7 classifier heads, and 3 segmentation regimes.
Compared radiomics and foundation-model features across 5 CT prediction tasks.
Evaluated worst-case cross-cohort performance on internal LUNG1 and external LUNG2 cohorts.
evidence_signal: Models were trained on LUNG1 (n=338) and tested on an internal set (n=84) plus external LUNG2 (n=211), with worst-case cross-cohort performance used as the primary benchmark.
watch_item: The reported radiomics advantage for some endpoints may partly reflect label-derivation effects, so results should be interpreted cautiously when transferring to new settings.
paper_title: Foundation Models vs. Radiomics for Lung Computed Tomography: A Benchmark of Feature Extractors, Classification Heads, and Segmentation Choices
